nativeRange.habitat: Lowland rainforest streams and coastal pools; also inhabits grass-plain streams with patchy rainforest cover. Found in the Kemp Welch and Collingwood river systems and coastal drainages between Port Moresby and Milne Bay.

decorPreferences: dense planting along sides and back (Java fern, Vallisneria, Hygrophila)
open central swimming lane for active mid-water shoaling
fine-leaved plants or spawning mops for egg deposition
subdued or dappled lighting to replicate rainforest stream canopy
dark substrate to intensify male coloration display

diet.notes: Parkinson's Rainbowfish accepts a wide variety of foods. High-quality flake, spirulina flake, and micro-pellets make a good staple diet. Frozen or live foods — brine shrimp, daphnia, mosquito larvae, bloodworms, or tubifex — offered two or three times per week add variety and encourage good colour. Wild fish feed on filamentous algae, insects, and invertebrates, so including vegetable-based and algae-enriched foods helps replicate that natural balance.

avoidWith: very small fish that could be outcompeted at feeding time
slow-moving long-finned species (fin harassment risk from male sparring)

imageCandidates.notes: Klaus Rudloff, Berlin; 1600x1068. Textbook display male: silver-blue front half transitioning to vivid golden-orange rear with orange dorsal/anal fins, sharp focus, whole fish framed against dark background. Small corner photographer credit only.

Phalinn Ooi; 3456x2304. Group/shoal shot showing the species' active social nature; central male shows dark body with vivid red-orange rear and red fins. Some motion blur and a busy display tank, so kept as a supporting image.
Shatilo G.V.; 3070x2047. Sharp full-body single specimen but a dull silvery-olive individual lacking the diagnostic golden-orange rear; useful angle/detail but off-colour, so non-recommended.

summary: Parkinson’s Rainbowfish is a robust, brilliantly coloured species endemic to coastal lowland streams of south-eastern Papua New Guinea. Display males develop a striking two-tone pattern: silver-blue on the front half and vivid golden-orange on the rear, with deep bodies and extended fins. Active and social, it is generally considered a good choice for beginners.
careNotes: Parkinson’s Rainbowfish (Melanotaenia parkinsoni) is endemic to the coastal lowland streams and pools of south-eastern Papua New Guinea, ranging from the Port Moresby area east to Milne Bay, within the Kemp Welch and Collingwood river catchments. In the wild these streams run through lowland rainforest and grass plains and tend toward warm, slightly alkaline, moderately hard conditions. A tank of at least 120 cm in length (roughly 210 L for a typical 120×45×40 cm footprint) is advisable for a shoal of six or more; the species is an active mid-water swimmer and uses the full horizontal length of the tank. Temperature should be maintained between 26–30°C and pH kept between 7.0–8.0 with moderate hardness. The species tolerates a wider pH range than many rainbowfish but does not do well in soft, acidic water. Good oxygenation is important — a power filter or surface agitation is recommended. Males tend to be most colourful when kept alongside females and rival males; sparring between males is generally harmless display behaviour rather than serious aggression. At sexual maturity, a male’s rear half transitions from silver-blue to a deep, reflective golden-orange, a coloration that is among the most distinctive in the family. The mouth is relatively narrow and the species is not a threat to tankmates of any normal community size. Ammonia and nitrite should be maintained at zero, so the species is best added only to a fully cycled aquarium. Weekly water changes of 25–30% are recommended to keep nitrates below 15 ppm.
breedingNotes: Parkinson’s Rainbowfish are continual egg-scatterers that will spawn over fine-leaved plants or spawning mops over many days. Eggs are adhesive and hatch in approximately 8–10 days depending on temperature, with warmer water shortening incubation. Both parents and tankmates will consume eggs and fry, so spawning mops are best transferred promptly to a dedicated rearing vessel filled with roughly 75% mature tank water and 25% fresh dechlorinated water. Fry are very small at hatching and require infusoria or commercial fry food initially, progressing to micro-worms and newly hatched brine shrimp within the first two weeks. Raising the water temperature toward the upper end of the range (28–30°C) tends to encourage spawning activity. Sexing adults is generally reliable: males are larger, deeper-bodied, and display the characteristic golden-orange rear coloration, while females are smaller and more uniformly silver-olive.

primary.name: FishBase — Melanotaenia parkinsoni
primary.notes: Primary taxonomic reference. Confirmed family Melanotaeniidae, order Atheriniformes, adult size males 11.0 cm SL / females 9.0 cm SL, native range Papua New Guinea (Kemp Welsh River to Milne Bay), habitat (lowland rainforest streams), temperature 26–30°C, pH 7.5–7.8, hardness ≥8 dH, trophic level 2.9 (omnivore), IUCN Near Threatened (2019). Also cross-referenced via FishBase summary ID 25638.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping (Fishkeeper.co.uk) — Parkinson’s Rainbowfish
additional.notes: Practical Fishkeeping UK species profile. Provided adult size 15 cm, temperature 26–30°C, pH 6.5–8.0, hardness up to 18 dH, minimum 4 ft tank, diet (flake, green flake, micropellets, frozen mosquito larvae and daphnia), breeding as continual spawners with 8–10 day egg hatch, and note that high O2 levels are required.

additional.name: AquaticCommunity — Parkinson’s Rainbowfish
additional.notes: Care overview. Confirmed temperature 27–30°C, alkaline pH ~7.6–7.8, minimum 13 cm adult size, habitat in coastal lowland streams and pools from Port Moresby to Milne Bay. Natural diet includes filamentous algae and insects. Noted egg hatch in ~10 days at recommended temperatures; fry require infusoria then brine shrimp nauplii.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net — Parkinson’s Rainbowfish
additional.notes: Additional care data. Provided adult size 10–11 cm, temperature 21–25°C (notably lower than other sources), pH 7–8, hardness 8–18°N, native range (Collingwood and Kemp Welch river systems), lifespan 5–7 years, swim zone (middle levels). Diet: high-quality flakes/micropellets plus frozen/live foods. Egg scatterers spawning over fine-leaved plants, hatch in 7–10 days.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Melanotaenia parkinsoni
additional.notes: Supplementary reference. Confirmed common name Orange Rainbowfish, described by Allen 1980, endemic to western Papua New Guinea (Kemp Welsh River and Milne Bay), adult size generally to 15 cm, dark lavender base coloration with rosy chest, males with deeper bodies, extended fins, and golden-orange rear half. IUCN Near Threatened.

fish.breedingNotes: Parkinson's Rainbowfish are continual egg-scatterers that typically spawn readily over fine-leaved plants or spawning mops at 28–30°C. Eggs hatch in approximately 8–10 days. The spawning medium should be moved to a separate rearing vessel to prevent adults from eating the eggs. Fry require infusoria or fine commercial fry food initially, transitioning to micro-worms and brine shrimp nauplii within the first couple of weeks.
